metadata.paper_title: Comparing the Maintainability of two Alternative Architectures of a Postal System: SOA vs. non-SOA
metadata.venue: 15th European Conference on Software Maintenance and Reengineering

system_context.system_description: A large non object oriented legacy system used by postal organizations that offer their customers specific services in order to manage big amounts of physical mail starting from electronic data files.
system_context.business_goals: Improve maintainability, reduce the time to close the change requests, and have a more agile IT system that can be adapted to change faster.

evaluation_setup.evaluation_goal: To compare two alternative architectures of the target postal system to understand whether a New Architecture based on SOA could reduce the maintenance/evolution times.

architectures.name: Actual Architecture
architectures.description: Reflects the real architecture of the current System. Electronic files are acquired by Front End, sent to Server, which creates SubServer processes. Batches are normalized, checked, cleaned, pre-generated, dispatched, generated and sent to Printing Centers.

key_components.responsibilities: Acquires electronic files (batches) submitted by customers and sends them to the Server.
Handles in parallel all received files; creates SubServer process for each batch.
Transforms batch inch by inch using Normalizer, Checker & Cleaner, and Pre-Generator & Generator.
Stores relevant information; contains Dispatcher Stored Procedures.

architectures.name: New Architecture (SOA)
architectures.description: Based on SOA principles. The Server manages the parallel execution of several running orchestrators (the Batches) which call Web services.

key_components.responsibilities: Manages parallel execution of running orchestrators (Batches).
Calls Web services to fulfill tasks.

assumptions: High cohesion and low coupling among components.

scenarios.scenario_text: Modifying the postal system so that it is able to accept and handle a new input batch format called C.

scenario_classification.rationale: Requires modification of existing components and addition of new components in both architectures to handle the new format.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Management of new Normalizer Type C, management of tool for format C, management of new Pre-Generator & Generator Type C, introduction of new type of flow.
Management of a new type of Batch/Input, introduction of new type of Batch, new Pre-Generator, new Generator.

architectural_strengths: New Architecture requires fewer changes (modifications plus additions) to accommodate scenarios.
New Architecture requires lower total effort.
architectural_weaknesses: Actual Architecture requires more changes (35 vs 27).
Actual Architecture requires higher effort (11 High/21 Med vs 8 High/8 Med).
overall_results.modifiability_assessment: Preliminary results are in favour of the New Architecture that can be adapted to change faster than the actual one.

architecture_comparison.rationale: Requires fewer changes (27 vs 35) and less effort (more 'Low' effort changes, fewer 'High' effort changes) to fit the considered ten scenarios.
Requires more component modifications and additions, and higher estimated effort for the evaluated scenarios.

recommendations: Proceed with migration towards SOA (re-engineering) if the comparison is completely in favour of the New Architecture.
Otherwise, perform restructuring at the level of code (refactoring).

missing_information: Full text of the other 9 scenarios (only new_input_format was detailed).
Specific authors' names are listed, but specific participants' count by role is not fully detailed.
inference_notes: Inferred 'Indirect' classification for the sample scenario because the paper lists specific component modifications and additions required.
Used 'Medium' and 'Low' effort estimates from Tables I and II to populate the scenario evaluation fields.
